Leaf_Symptoms: Small to large brown spots with concentric rings creating target-spot appearance starting on older lower leaves; lesions may coalesce causing complete yellowing and defoliation
Tuber_Symptoms: Dark sunken circular to irregular lesions on tubers with dry corky brown flesh underneath; lesions often surrounded by raised violet-blue border

Leaf_Symptoms: Small, dark brown necrotic spots (up to 10 mm diameter) that may coalesce into larger necrotic areas, lacking prominent concentric rings. Severely affected foliage may dry up and senesce prematurely
Tuber_Symptoms: Deep, black, sunken pits (black pit) with definite margins that often develop in storage, particularly on tubers damaged during harvest

Leaf_Symptoms: Initial symptoms include vein clearing and leaf epinasty followed by stunting, yellowing of lower leaves, progressive wilting, defoliation, and plant death. A diagnostic feature is brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible in stem cross-sections.
Tuber_Symptoms: Dry, firm, circular, light to dark brown lesions on tuber surface that may shrink and dry out. Infection usually limited to cortex, does not typically extend beyond vascular ring.

Leaf_Symptoms: Leaf symptoms typically absent or include wilting and leaf dehydration caused by fungal toxins transported through vascular system; foliar symptoms more common with other Fusarium species like F. oxysporum
Tuber_Symptoms: Dry rot on tuber surface appears as brown shriveled tissue with concentric streaks, sometimes showing pinkish to whitish mycelium; cut tubers reveal brown rot progressing into flesh with potential internal cavities
Reference_Source: Le plant de pomme de terre français

Leaf_Symptoms: Leaf symptoms typically not observed or are secondary; primarily a tuber and storage pathogen
Tuber_Symptoms: Tuber surface shows brown sunken lesions that shrivel; internal dry rot with brown discoloration and possible cavities; pinkish-white mycelium may appear under humid conditions
Reference_Source: Le plant de pomme de terre français

Leaf_Symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis and necrosis beginning on lower leaves, often with V-shaped lesions; wilting and premature vine death occur as disease progresses with symptoms more severe on warm sunny days
Tuber_Symptoms: None typically; vascular ring at stem end may show light brown to tan discoloration in some cultivars
Global_Distribution: Worldwide in temperate and warm production regions
East_Africa_Presence: Confirmed in Kenya; reported in Uganda and Tanzania; likely present in Ethiopia

Leaf_Symptoms: Reddish-brown to brown, sunken cankers on underground stems and stolons. Aboveground symptoms include yellowing, purpling, upward curling of leaves, and stunted growth. In severe cases, aerial tubers form on stems.
Tuber_Symptoms: Black, irregularly shaped, hard masses (sclerotia) on the tuber surface, often described as 'dirt that won't wash off'. Superficial and does not damage flesh but affects marketability. Can cause misshapen tubers and netting or elephant hide on skin.

Leaf_Symptoms: Small water-soaked lesions turn dark brown to black typically associated with wounds from windblown soil; infected plants may wilt with lower and middle leaves becoming chlorotic; premature death of haulms with leaves yellowing and curling upward.
Tuber_Symptoms: Silvery to grey irregular lesions on tuber surface with characteristic black microsclerotia (black dots) visible within lesions; lesions may appear brown with poorly defined margin; deep sunken lesions may develop under cold storage.
East_Africa_Presence: Confirmed in Ethiopia, Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Sudan, and South Africa
Reference_Source: CABI Distribution Maps of Plant Diseases

Leaf_Symptoms: Water-soaked, light to dark brown or blackish lesions on leaves often with pale green borders that expand rapidly; white sporulation (sporangia) appears at lesion margins on underside of leaves under humid conditions.
Tuber_Symptoms: Firm, dark purplish-brown to copper brown sunken lesions on tuber surface; internally the infected flesh appears dry, reddish-brown to granular extending into flesh; often secondary bacterial soft rot follows.

Leaf_Symptoms: No foliar symptoms - pathogen only affects potato tubers
Tuber_Symptoms: Surface blemishes that are tan to gray to black irregular lesions; when wet, lesions have silvery appearance; can coalesce to cover large portion of tuber; may cause shrinkage and water loss in storage

Leaf_Symptoms: Generally symptomless on foliage; superficial brown cracked lesions may develop on stems and stolons that coalesce into larger brown patches with deep longitudinal cracks
Tuber_Symptoms: Small, dark grey to black, slightly raised pustules (0.5-4 mm) often surrounded by a sunken ring; underlying corky tissue extends up to 2mm into tuber

Leaf_Symptoms: No visible symptoms on foliage unless vectoring Potato mop-top virus (PMTV). Root galls form on roots and stolons which can impair plant growth.
Tuber_Symptoms: Small, purplish-brown raised pustules on tuber surface that rupture to release powdery brown spore balls. Lesions can merge into large scabby areas; tubers may become misshapen.
East_Africa_Presence: Confirmed in Ethiopia and across East Africa

Leaf_Symptoms: Seeds may rot before germination or seedlings collapse at soil line with water-soaked stem tissue (damping-off). Older plants show stunting, yellowing, wilting, and brown water-soaked root rot with cortex sloughing off.
Tuber_Symptoms: Tuber symptoms include 'Pythium leak' - infected tubers show grey to brown water-soaked lesions with distinct margins; internal flesh becomes grey, spongy, and leaks clear liquid when cut; tubers may have a vinegar-like odor.
East_Africa_Presence: Confirmed in Sudan and Algeria; reported in Egypt and Ethiopia

Leaf_Symptoms: Initial water-soaked lesions on stems and leaves that develop into soft rot with white cottony mycelial growth. Infected tissues collapse and die; small black sclerotia form on and inside infected plant parts.
Tuber_Symptoms: Tubers develop soft watery rot with white mycelial growth on surface and black sclerotia may form. Infected tubers become spongy and eventually collapse.
East_Africa_Presence: Confirmed in Ethiopia, Kenya, Sudan, Tanzania, and Uganda

Leaf_Symptoms: Infected leaves lose vitality, turn yellow, wilt, and die, typically during hot dry weather. Small brown to black lesions may form on leaves.
Tuber_Symptoms: Gray pigmentation around lenticels advancing to dark brown or black patches on skin surface. Internal tuber tissue shows dark discoloration.
East_Africa_Presence: Confirmed in Ethiopia, Kenya, Sudan, Tanzania, Uganda

Disease_Name: Phoma leaf spot, Phoma blight, Black root rot
Leaf_Symptoms: Small, irregular, pale brown spots (1-2 mm) with dark brown borders on leaves and stems; lesions may coalesce and cause blighting of foliage.
Tuber_Symptoms: None specific to tubers for this pathogen; primarily a foliar and stem pathogen. In other hosts, can cause black root rot but not a primary potato tuber pathogen.
Global_Distribution: Worldwide - Cosmopolitan distribution on diverse plant hosts including capsicum, tomato, bean, pea, and numerous weed species.
